Dates on each article: 21 July 2026 / 2026-07-21, matching the actual run date, in both the visible byline and the JSON-LD datePublished/dateModified, continuing correct per-run dating across nineteen consecutive runs now. Date-accuracy audit run this session per a standing client note that articles 'are all published as June 16th': checked every file for a literal '16 June 2026' or '2026-06-16' date and cross-referenced each hit against its true git first-commit date. Result: the only 21 files carrying that date are the original site/services/ pillar and money launch pages (seq 1-39 subset), and all 21 were genuinely first committed and published on 16 June 2026 as a single launch batch, confirmed via `git log --follow --diff-filter=A`; that date is factually correct for those pages, not a bug, and rewriting it would falsify the record and risk a Google Search Console date-manipulation flag. A second check of 9 June-dated site/blog/ authority posts (cac-and-ltv, crm-implementation-project-plan, csat-explained, founder-stage-growth, fractional-revenue-leadership, how-to-calculate-conversion-rate, types-of-market-entry-strategies, what-is-a-good-conversion-rate, what-is-conversion-rate-optimisation) found they already carry their correct individual 27-29 June 2026 dates, matching their git commit dates exactly; no fix was needed there either. Conclusion: the per-run dating mechanism has no live bug; the client's flagged pages are evergreen and correctly retain their true original publish date, same as any editorial site. Flagged back to the client in this run's Slack message and worth closing out as a non-issue rather than re-flagging on future runs. Slack link format changed this run: discovered the slack_send_message MCP tool's own description states it renders **standard Markdown** (including `[text](url)` links), not Slack's native `<url|text>` mrkdwn syntax; last run's native-syntax convention was very likely the actual cause of the reported bleed, since feeding raw mrkdwn into a standard-Markdown renderer can leave the trailing `|Title>` unconsumed and merged into adjacent text. Switched to `[Title](url)` standard Markdown links, one per line, three lines in a single message, matching the routine's literal instructions; the closing `)` immediately follows `.html` with nothing unescaped trailing it, which should eliminate the bleed. Verify against how this actually renders in Slack before treating it as settled; if it recurs, the next escalation is one plain unformatted message per article with only the bare URL as the entire message body.
